title: Machine Learning & AI Fundamentals for Government Data
summary: How machines learn from data, the main algorithm families, how models are trained and evaluated, and the ethics, bias, and explainability concerns that matter for government use.
content:
Machine learning (ML) is a branch of AI where systems learn patterns from data rather than following hand-coded rules. The three broad paradigms are: supervised learning (learns from labeled examples, e.g. past visa cases tagged approved/denied), unsupervised learning (finds structure in unlabeled data, e.g. grouping travellers by behaviour), and reinforcement learning (an agent learns by trial-and-error against rewards, e.g. optimising a routing or scheduling policy).

Supervised tasks split into classification (predict a category — fraud / not fraud) and regression (predict a number — expected visa processing time). Unsupervised work is dominated by clustering (group similar records) and dimensionality reduction (compress many features). The model learns a mapping from features (input variables — applicant age, country, travel history) to a label or target (the answer you want to predict).

• Concept-level algorithm map: Linear regression fits a straight-line relationship to predict a number; logistic regression predicts a probability for a yes/no class. Decision trees split data on if-then rules; random forests average many trees to cut overfitting. k-means clusters points around k centroids (unsupervised). kNN classifies a new point by its nearest labelled neighbours. Neural networks stack layers of weighted connections; deep learning uses many layers for images, speech and text. Large language models (LLMs) are deep networks trained to predict the next token, powering generative AI that drafts, summarises and translates.

To know whether a model truly learned, data is split: a training set to fit the model, a validation set to tune settings, and a held-out test set to estimate real-world performance. Overfitting means the model memorised the training data and fails on new data; underfitting means it is too simple to capture the pattern. This is the bias-variance tradeoff: high bias = underfit, high variance = overfit; the goal is the balance that generalises.

• Evaluation for classification uses a confusion matrix of true/false positives and negatives. Accuracy = fraction correct, but it misleads on imbalanced data (rare fraud). Precision = of those flagged, how many were right; recall (sensitivity) = of the true cases, how many were caught; F1 is their harmonic mean. ROC curves plot true-positive vs false-positive rates across thresholds, and AUC summarises that as a single 0.5–1.0 score. Cross-validation (e.g. k-fold) rotates the test split to get a stabler estimate on limited data.

A production ML pipeline runs: data collection → cleaning and feature engineering → train/validate/test → deploy → monitor. Over time model drift sets in — the world changes (new travel patterns, new fraud tactics) and accuracy decays, so models need retraining. MLOps applies DevOps discipline (versioning, automated retraining, monitoring, rollback) to keep models reliable.

For government and diplomatic use, AI ethics is not optional. Models trained on biased historical decisions can reproduce discrimination (e.g. unfairly flagging applicants by nationality). Explainability/interpretability matters because affected people may have a right to know why a decision was made, and officials must be accountable. Best practice keeps a human in the loop for consequential decisions, documents data sources and limitations, tests for disparate impact across groups, and treats opaque 'black-box' models with caution where rights are at stake.

• Generative AI (LLMs) can boost drafting, translation and research, but can 'hallucinate' confident falsehoods, leak sensitive inputs, and must never be fed classified or personal data into untrusted services — verification and data-handling rules apply.
keyPoints: Three ML paradigms: supervised (labeled data), unsupervised (unlabeled/structure-finding), reinforcement (reward-driven trial and error).
Classification predicts categories, regression predicts numbers, clustering groups similar unlabeled records.
Features are inputs; labels/targets are the answers the model learns to predict.
Data is split into training (fit), validation (tune), and test (final unbiased evaluation) sets.
Overfitting = memorises training data (high variance); underfitting = too simple (high bias); the bias-variance tradeoff seeks the generalising balance.
Confusion matrix yields accuracy, precision, recall, and F1; ROC/AUC assess classifiers across thresholds; accuracy misleads on imbalanced data.
Cross-validation (k-fold) gives a more reliable performance estimate on limited data.
Model drift degrades accuracy as conditions change; MLOps handles retraining, monitoring, versioning, and rollback.
Government AI must address bias, fairness, explainability, accountability, and keep humans in the loop for high-stakes decisions.
Generative AI/LLMs can hallucinate and leak data; never feed classified or personal data into untrusted models.

flashcards.back: Supervised learns from labeled examples (known answers) to predict; unsupervised finds structure/patterns in unlabeled data (e.g. clustering).
Classification predicts a discrete category (fraud/not fraud); regression predicts a continuous number (processing time, cost).
An agent learns by trial and error, taking actions and receiving rewards/penalties to maximise long-term reward (e.g. routing, scheduling).
Features are the input variables fed to a model; the label (target) is the correct answer the model is trained to predict.
Overfitting memorises training data and fails on new data (high variance); underfitting is too simple to capture the pattern (high bias).
Balancing a model's simplicity (bias/underfit) against its sensitivity to training data (variance/overfit) to maximise generalisation.
Train fits the model, validation tunes hyperparameters, and the held-out test set gives an unbiased estimate of real-world performance.
Precision = of items flagged positive, how many were correct; recall = of all true positives, how many were caught. F1 balances both.
A table of true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives used to compute accuracy, precision, recall, and F1.
ROC plots true-positive vs false-positive rate across thresholds; AUC summarises it as one score (0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect).
On imbalanced data (e.g. rare fraud), a model predicting the majority class scores high accuracy while missing the cases that matter.
Rotating the data into k folds, training on k-1 and testing on the rest each time, to get a stabler performance estimate on limited data.
A decision tree splits data on if-then rules; a random forest averages many varied trees to reduce overfitting and improve accuracy.
Decay in model accuracy over time as real-world data/conditions shift away from the training data, requiring monitoring and retraining (MLOps).

flashcards.front: What is an LLM / generative AI?
flashcards.back: A deep neural network trained to predict the next token, generating text/translation/summaries; can 'hallucinate' false but confident output.
Bias/fairness, explainability, accountability, human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions, and never exposing personal/classified data to untrusted models.

title: Business Intelligence, Metrics & Reporting
summary: How organisations turn data into KPIs, dashboards and reports, the four levels of analytics in action, and how to design honest metrics and avoid misleading visuals.
content:
Business intelligence (BI) is the discipline and tooling that turns raw data into information for decisions — collecting, modelling and presenting it through dashboards and reports. In common usage 'analytics' leans toward deeper investigation and prediction, while 'BI' leans toward describing and monitoring what is happening; in practice they overlap on the same data foundation.

• The four analytics types build on each other: descriptive (what happened — visas issued last quarter), diagnostic (why it happened — backlog rose because of a staffing gap), predictive (what will happen — forecast next quarter's demand), and prescriptive (what to do — reallocate officers to the busiest posts). Each step adds value and complexity.

A metric is any quantified measurement (number of applications received). A KPI (Key Performance Indicator) is a metric tied to a goal that signals success or failure (e.g. percentage of visas processed within the service standard). All KPIs are metrics, but only a few metrics deserve KPI status — too many 'key' indicators means none are key. Distinguish leading indicators (predictive, act early — incoming application volume) from lagging indicators (confirm results after the fact — last month's processing time).

• Delivery formats differ in purpose: dashboards give an at-a-glance, often real-time view of KPIs for monitoring; reports are structured, periodic, detailed documents for the record; ad-hoc analysis answers a one-off question on demand. Self-service BI tools (Power BI, Tableau and similar) let non-technical staff build their own visuals and explore data without writing code, reducing the bottleneck on analysts — within governed data sources.

Interactive exploration relies on a few core moves: drill-down (go from a summary to finer detail, e.g. region → country → post), roll-up (the reverse), slice (filter to one value of a dimension), and dice (filter on several dimensions at once). These operate over OLAP cubes — multidimensional structures where measures (the numbers, e.g. application counts) are analysed across dimensions (time, location, visa type).

• A semantic layer sits between raw tables and users: it defines business-friendly names and consistent metric definitions (one agreed definition of 'active case') so everyone querying the data gets the same answer. This is central to a single source of truth and avoids teams reporting different numbers for the same thing.

Data storytelling is presenting findings as a clear narrative — context, the key insight, and the 'so what' — rather than dumping charts. Equally important is avoiding misleading visuals: truncated y-axes that exaggerate change, dual axes that imply false correlation, cherry-picked time ranges, 3-D effects that distort proportions, and pie charts with too many slices. Honest charts start bar axes at zero, label clearly, and match the chart type to the question.

• Reporting also needs governance and distribution discipline: control who can see which data (especially sensitive or personal data), schedule and version reports, and ensure access aligns with classification and need-to-know. A good metric is SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) and, above all, actionable — it should change a decision. Beware vanity metrics that look impressive but drive no action, and 'gaming' where staff optimise the metric instead of the underlying goal.
keyPoints: BI focuses on describing/monitoring; analytics extends to deeper investigation and prediction — both share the same data foundation.
Four analytics levels: descriptive (what), diagnostic (why), predictive (what will), prescriptive (what to do).
A metric is any measurement; a KPI is a metric tied to a goal — all KPIs are metrics, but few metrics are KPIs.
Leading indicators predict and let you act early; lagging indicators confirm outcomes after they occur.
Dashboards monitor KPIs at a glance (often real-time); reports are periodic and detailed; ad-hoc answers one-off questions.
Self-service BI (Power BI, Tableau) lets non-technical users explore governed data without coding.
OLAP cubes hold measures (numbers) analysed across dimensions (time, place, type); drill-down, roll-up, slice and dice navigate them.
A semantic layer defines consistent, business-friendly metric definitions to create a single source of truth.
Avoid misleading visuals: truncated/zero-violating axes, dual axes, cherry-picked ranges, 3-D distortion, overloaded pie charts.
Good metrics are SMART and actionable; beware vanity metrics and metric-gaming; report access must follow governance and need-to-know.

flashcards.back: BI describes and monitors what is happening (dashboards/reports); analytics digs deeper into why and what will happen (diagnostic/predictive). They overlap on shared data.
Descriptive (what happened), diagnostic (why), predictive (what will happen), prescriptive (what action to take).
A metric is any quantified measurement; a KPI is a metric tied to a strategic goal that signals success/failure. All KPIs are metrics; few metrics are KPIs.
Leading indicators are predictive and let you act early (incoming volume); lagging indicators confirm results after the fact (last month's output).
Dashboard = at-a-glance, often real-time KPI monitoring; report = structured periodic detail for the record; ad-hoc = one-off answer on demand.
Tools like Power BI/Tableau that let non-technical users build visuals and explore governed data without writing code, easing the analyst bottleneck.
A multidimensional data structure where measures (numbers) are analysed across dimensions (time, location, type) for fast slicing.
Drill-down moves from summary to detail; slice filters to one value of a dimension; dice filters on several dimensions at once.
Measures are the numeric values being analysed (counts, amounts); dimensions are the categories you analyse them by (time, region, type).
A business-friendly abstraction over raw tables that fixes consistent names and metric definitions, giving everyone one agreed answer (single source of truth).
Presenting findings as a clear narrative — context, key insight, and 'so what' — instead of dumping disconnected charts.
Truncated y-axes, dual axes implying false correlation, cherry-picked time ranges, 3-D distortion, and pie charts with too many slices.

flashcards.front: Why should bar-chart axes start at zero?
flashcards.back: Truncating the axis exaggerates small differences, visually misleading the reader about the true magnitude of change.
Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound — and, above all, actionable so it actually changes a decision.
A metric that looks impressive (e.g. total page views) but drives no decision or action; contrast with actionable metrics.
When people optimise the measured number rather than the underlying goal, distorting behaviour (Goodhart's law).

title: Data Engineering, Big-Data Tech & Governance in Practice
summary: How data pipelines are architected and run at scale — batch vs stream, file formats, orchestration and catalogs — plus the governance roles, regulations and security that keep embassy data safe and compliant.
content:
Data engineering builds the plumbing that delivers reliable data to analysts and models. A pipeline has four stages: ingestion (pull data in from source systems, APIs, files), storage (land it in a lake, warehouse or lakehouse), processing (clean, join, transform), and serving (expose it to dashboards, queries and ML). Good architecture separates these so each can scale and fail independently.

• Two processing modes coexist. Batch processing handles large volumes on a schedule — the classic stack is Hadoop (distributed storage via HDFS) with MapReduce, now largely superseded by Apache Spark, which is much faster because it processes in memory. Stream processing handles events continuously, low-latency, as they arrive — Apache Kafka transports event streams, and Kafka Streams or Apache Flink compute on them in real time (e.g. live border-crossing or alert feeds).

The lakehouse combines the cheap, flexible storage of a data lake with the structure, transactions and performance of a warehouse, so one platform serves both raw exploration and governed reporting. Underneath, how files are stored matters: columnar formats like Parquet and ORC store data by column, so analytical queries read only the columns they need — far faster and smaller than row-based formats. Compression and partitioning (splitting data by date, region, etc.) cut scan costs and speed queries by letting engines skip irrelevant data.

• Pipelines must run in the right order and recover from failure, which is the job of orchestration. Tools like Apache Airflow model workflows as DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs) — tasks with dependencies and no cycles — scheduling them, retrying failures, and alerting on problems. A data catalog with rich metadata (what each dataset is, where it came from, its lineage, owner and sensitivity) makes data discoverable and trustworthy.

Organisational models for data differ. Centralised data management puts one team in charge of a single platform — consistent but can bottleneck. A data mesh decentralises ownership to domain teams who treat their data as a product, with federated governance — better for large, complex organisations but harder to coordinate. Master Data Management (MDM) maintains one authoritative, deduplicated record for core entities (a person, a post, an organisation) so 'the same person' is not stored five different ways.

• Trust in data requires data quality and validation (checks for completeness, accuracy, consistency, timeliness, uniqueness, validity) plus data observability — continuously monitoring pipelines for freshness, volume anomalies, schema changes and broken lineage, so issues are caught before they reach reports. Governance assigns clear roles: a data owner is accountable for a dataset and its use; a data steward defines and enforces its quality and meaning day-to-day; and a data custodian (often IT) handles the technical storage, access and protection.

The regulatory landscape constrains how data — especially personal data — is handled. The GDPR governs personal data of EU individuals (lawful basis, data-subject rights, breach notification, cross-border transfer rules); national instruments like a Privacy Act impose similar duties; and records-retention schedules dictate how long official records must be kept and when they must be destroyed. For a foreign ministry, cross-border data transfer, classification, and diplomatic/consular confidentiality add further obligations.

• Security underpins all of it. Protect data at rest with encryption on disk/databases and strict access control, and data in transit with TLS/encrypted channels. Apply least-privilege access, audit logging, and need-to-know aligned to classification. Treat personal and classified embassy data — visa records, consular files, communications — as high-sensitivity throughout the pipeline, from ingestion to deletion.
keyPoints: A data pipeline has four stages: ingestion, storage, processing, and serving — kept loosely coupled so each scales independently.
Batch (Hadoop/MapReduce, now Spark for in-memory speed) processes scheduled bulk data; streaming (Kafka + Kafka Streams/Flink) processes events in real time.
A lakehouse merges a lake's cheap flexible storage with a warehouse's structure, transactions, and performance.
Columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) plus compression and partitioning make analytical queries faster and cheaper by reading only needed data.
Orchestration tools like Airflow model pipelines as DAGs — dependency-ordered, acyclic tasks with scheduling, retries, and alerting.
A data catalog with metadata and lineage makes datasets discoverable and trustworthy.
Centralised data management is consistent but can bottleneck; a data mesh gives domain teams product ownership with federated governance.
Master Data Management (MDM) maintains one authoritative, deduplicated record for core entities like a person or organisation.
Governance roles: owner (accountable), steward (defines/enforces quality and meaning), custodian (technical storage and protection).
Comply with GDPR/Privacy Act and records-retention rules; encrypt data at rest and in transit, and enforce least-privilege, need-to-know access.

flashcards.front: Four stages of a data pipeline
flashcards.back: Ingestion (bring data in), storage (land it), processing (clean/transform), serving (expose to dashboards, queries, ML).
Batch processes large volumes on a schedule (Spark/Hadoop); streaming processes events continuously and low-latency as they arrive (Kafka/Flink).
Hadoop+MapReduce is the classic disk-based distributed batch stack; Spark is its faster successor, processing largely in memory.

flashcards.front: What is Apache Kafka used for?
flashcards.back: A distributed log/messaging platform that transports high-throughput event streams; Kafka Streams or Flink compute on those streams in real time.
A platform combining a data lake's cheap, flexible raw storage with a warehouse's structure, transactions, and query performance.

flashcards.front: Why use columnar formats like Parquet/ORC?
flashcards.back: They store data by column, so analytical queries read only the needed columns — much faster and smaller than row-based formats, and compress well.
Splits data by a key (date, region) so query engines skip irrelevant partitions, cutting scan cost and speeding queries.

flashcards.front: What is a DAG in orchestration?
flashcards.back: A Directed Acyclic Graph of tasks with dependencies and no cycles; tools like Airflow schedule, retry, and alert on them.
A searchable inventory of datasets with metadata (definitions, source, lineage, owner, sensitivity) that makes data discoverable and trustworthy.

flashcards.front: Data mesh vs centralised data management
flashcards.back: Centralised = one team/platform, consistent but can bottleneck; data mesh = domain teams own their data as a product under federated governance.
Maintaining a single authoritative, deduplicated record for core entities (person, organisation, location) to avoid conflicting duplicates.
Continuous monitoring of pipelines for freshness, volume anomalies, schema changes, and broken lineage to catch issues before they hit reports.

flashcards.front: Data owner vs steward vs custodian
flashcards.back: Owner is accountable for the dataset; steward defines and enforces its quality/meaning day-to-day; custodian (IT) handles technical storage and protection.
Processing of EU individuals' personal data — lawful basis, data-subject rights, breach notification, and cross-border transfer rules.
Rules dictating how long official records must be kept and when they must be securely destroyed.

flashcards.front: Data at rest vs in transit security
flashcards.back: At rest: encrypt stored data (disk/DB) with access controls; in transit: encrypt moving data with TLS/secure channels. Apply least-privilege throughout.
